title: SEGMENT 8 — The 14-day rule: where the ethical limit came from

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — The 14-day rule: where the ethical limit came from
To understand the ethics of synthetic embryos, you need to understand the history of human embryo research ethics, which centers on something called the 14-day rule.
This is the rule, agreed upon in most of the developed world for decades, that human embryos cannot be cultured in research labs beyond fourteen days after fertilization.
Where did it come from?
Why fourteen days?
And why is it now under pressure?

The 14-day rule emerged in the late 1970s and early 1980s, in the wake of the first successful IVF birth — Louise Brown, born in 1978 — which suddenly made human embryos available for research in larger numbers than ever before.
Before IVF, human embryos in research labs were extremely rare — occasional specimens from miscarriages, ectopic pregnancies, abortions.
After IVF, there were spare embryos created during fertility treatment, some of which were donated for research.
This created an urgent need for clear ethical guidelines: what kinds of research were permissible?
Up to what point?
Under what oversight?
In the UK, the Warnock Committee was convened in 1982 to address these questions.
Chaired by the philosopher Mary Warnock, the committee published its report in 1984.
It recommended, among other things, that human embryos could be used for research, with strict licensing, up to a specific developmental milestone: the appearance of the primitive streak.

The primitive streak is a thickening on the embryonic disc that appears around day 14 to 15 of human development, and it marks the beginning of gastrulation — the moment when the three germ layers form and the body axes are definitively established.
It's also, importantly, the last point at which identical twinning can occur.
Before the primitive streak forms, an embryo can still split into two embryos.
After the primitive streak, it cannot — the embryo becomes a single, individual, definitively-one-body developmental unit.
The choice of the primitive streak was not random.
It reflected a particular ethical reasoning.
Before the primitive streak, the embryo is a cluster of cells that hasn't yet committed to being one individual.
It can still twin.
It can still fail.
It is a precursor to individuality but isn't yet individual.
After the primitive streak, the embryo is, in some morally relevant sense, a unique developing organism.
The Warnock Committee argued that this transition was a defensible place to draw a line.
Fourteen days post-fertilization is when the primitive streak appears, in the typical case.
So the recommendation became: research on human embryos is permitted up to day 14 or the appearance of the primitive streak, whichever comes first.
This recommendation was codified into UK law in the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act of 1990.

Similar rules were adopted in most other countries that permitted any embryo research at all — through legislation in some places, through professional society guidelines in others, through institutional review board policies elsewhere.
By the 1990s, the 14-day rule was the global norm.
The International Society for Stem Cell Research, the ISSCR — the main professional body for this work — included it as a strict prohibition in their guidelines.
A few important nuances about the 14-day rule, because they bear on what comes next.
It was always partly pragmatic.
For most of the history of the rule, you couldn't grow a human embryo in a lab to fourteen days even if you wanted to.
The technology simply wasn't there.
Embryos in IVF labs were typically transferred to a uterus or frozen by day five or six — the blastocyst stage.
Beyond that, they died.
So the 14-day rule was, for decades, a theoretical limit.
Nobody was bumping up against it because nobody could get close.
The line was always somewhat arbitrary.
The Warnock Committee acknowledged this.
The primitive streak is a real biological milestone, but ethical reasoning about embryos doesn't reduce to "is the primitive streak present.
" You could have drawn the line earlier — at the blastocyst, say — or later — at the heartbeat, or at recognizable brain activity, or at viability outside the womb.
The 14-day point was chosen because it was a clear, biologically grounded milestone that had defensible moral significance.
But other defensible lines could have been drawn.
It applied to embryos made by fertilization.
This is crucial.
The 14-day rule was written in 1984, decades before synthetic embryos existed.
The drafters were thinking about embryos created by sperm fertilizing egg.
The question of whether the rule applied to embryos created some other way — by somatic cell nuclear transfer, for example, or by stem cell assembly — wasn't on the radar.
The rule was about preimplantation human embryos as they had existed for thousands of years, just now in a laboratory dish.
That last nuance is what's been blown apart by synthetic embryos.
If a structure is made from stem cells, with no fertilization event, does the 14-day rule apply at all?
If it does, then the developmental clock has to be inferred — how do you measure "days post-fertilization" for an embryo that wasn't fertilized?
If it doesn't, then synthetic embryos exist outside the entire ethical framework that was built for embryo research, and a new framework has to be constructed.
The 14-day rule, then, is both a real ethical constraint and a historical artifact.
It represents decades of careful moral reasoning about when an embryo becomes individuated, when it gains additional moral status, when research limitations should kick in.
But it was built for a different kind of object than the one synthetic embryos represent.
As long as the rule was a theoretical limit — as long as nobody could grow a real embryo close to fourteen days anyway — the question of how to apply it to synthetic embryos didn't matter much.
But starting around 2016, the technology to grow real human embryos in dishes started to improve.

Magdalena Zernicka-Goetz's group and Ali Brivanlou's group, working in parallel, developed culture systems that allowed real human embryos donated from IVF to be cultured in dishes for substantially longer than before — close to the 14-day limit.
And in the same window, synthetic embryos became real.

Suddenly the 14-day rule, which had been a theoretical limit for thirty years, was both a practical limit (because real embryos could now reach it) and a deeply ambiguous limit (because synthetic embryos didn't clearly fall under it).
The field, by 2020 or so, needed an updated framework.
That's where the 2021 ISSCR guidelines come in.
